suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Stories that explore fairness from different angles
[Look Together] Examples of rules in different settings

competence_markers.description: The student can identify beliefs behind rules — recognizing that rules come from beliefs about what is important
The student understands the difference between science as a tool and science as a worldview
The student can articulate that "progress" is a belief, not an established fact
The student can describe different concepts of fairness and identify the beliefs behind each
The student has begun to identify beliefs they hold and to ask where those beliefs came from
The student understands that everyone has beliefs about right and wrong — including people who say they do not

narration_prompts.prompt: Give me an example of a rule and tell me what belief is behind it.
narration_prompts.detail: Why does that rule exist? What does the person who made the rule believe about what is important?

narration_prompts.prompt: What can science tell us? What can it not tell us?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example of a question science can answer and a question it cannot answer.
Better in what ways? Worse in what ways? What does "better" depend on?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about three different ways to understand fairness.
narration_prompts.detail: What belief is behind each one? Which one do you think is most fair — and why?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is a belief you hold that most people around you also hold?
narration_prompts.detail: Where did this belief come from? Could someone disagree with you and still be a good person?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between a fact and a belief?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example of each. Can you give me an example of something most people treat as a fact but is actually a belief?

cross_connections.description: This packet is the practical application of 1.3.1's framework to the student's own life and culture.
Every tradition answers the questions raised here. The secular answers are one more set of answers, not the default.
Rules and laws come from beliefs about right and wrong. The connection is direct.
The beliefs the student examines here are the foundations of their own moral development.

content.why_this_matters:
In this curriculum, you have studied what many different peoples believe about God, the world, and how people should live. You have met the gods of Greece and Scandinavia. You have learned about the orishas of the Yoruba and the Dreaming of Aboriginal Australia. You have studied Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, and Buddhism.

But there is one set of beliefs we have not yet examined — and it may be the most important one of all, because it is the one most likely to be invisible to you. These are the beliefs held by people who say they do not have any religious beliefs at all.

Some people believe that science can explain everything. Some people believe that human progress is inevitable — that the world is always getting better. Some people believe that human beings can solve all their problems through technology. Some people believe that the most important thing in the world is equality, or freedom, or being true to yourself.

These are beliefs. They are not facts. They are things people believe because they have a worldview — a way of seeing the world — even if they have never thought about it that way. And just like every other worldview in 1.3, these beliefs shape how people live, what they value, and what they are willing to fight for.

In this packet, you will begin to learn that everyone has beliefs about what matters most — even people who say they "just believe in science" or "don't believe in anything." Those statements are themselves beliefs. And understanding them is just as important as understanding any religion.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The realization that everyone has beliefs** — even people who say they do not
2. **The difference between science as a tool and science as a worldview** — science is a way of investigating things; scientism is the belief that only science can reveal truth
3. **The idea that "progress" is a belief, not a fact** — the assumption that the world is always getting better is something you have to believe; it is not self-evident
4. **Rules come from beliefs** — every family, every school, every country has rules, and those rules come from beliefs about what is right and wrong
5. **Fairness means different things to different people** — and the differences come from different beliefs about what matters most
6. **The habit of asking "What does this person believe?"** — even when the person does not think they have beliefs
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Everyone Has Rules — But Not the Same Rules

You already know that different families have different rules. Some families say "no screen time on weekdays." Other families say "screen time is fine after homework." Some families say "always clean your plate." Others say "eat until you are full."

Why are the rules different? Because the families believe different things about what is important. The family that limits screen time believes that too much screen time is bad for children. The family that allows it believes that children should learn to manage their own time. Neither family is obviously wrong. They start from different beliefs — and the beliefs produce different rules.

Now think bigger. Countries have rules too — laws. Why are the laws different in different countries? Because the people who make the laws believe different things about human nature, about fairness, and about what a good society looks like.

This is the most important idea in this packet: **rules do not come from nowhere. They come from beliefs. And beliefs are things people choose to believe, even when they do not realize they are choosing.**

### Part 2: "I Just Believe in Science"

You may have heard someone say, "I don't believe in religion — I just believe in science." This sounds like it means the person does not have beliefs. But it actually means something very specific: the person believes that the scientific method is the best (or only) way to understand the world.

Science is wonderful. It is one of the most powerful tools human beings have ever developed. It has cured diseases, put people on the moon, and explained how the universe works at levels our ancestors could never have imagined.

But science is a tool for investigating certain kinds of questions. It can tell you what happens when you mix chemicals. It can tell you how fast light travels. It can tell you how old a fossil is. What it cannot tell you is:

- **Whether your life has meaning.** That is not a question science can answer.
- **Whether it is wrong to steal.** Science can tell you that stealing exists; it cannot tell you it is wrong.
- **Whether beauty matters.** Science can measure wavelengths of light; it cannot tell you why a sunset moves you.
- **Whether you should be kind to people who are mean to you.** That is a moral question, and science does not do morals.

The person who says "I just believe in science" is actually saying: "I believe that the physical world is the most important thing, and that the scientific method is the best way to know things." That is a belief. It is not a fact that science has proven. It is a worldview — a way of seeing the world that shapes everything else.

### Part 3: Is the World Getting Better?

Many people believe that the world is getting better — that over time, things improve. Diseases get cured. Slavery gets abolished. People get more rights. Technology makes life easier. This idea is called "progress," and many people treat it as a fact.

But is it a fact — or a belief?

In some ways, the world has gotten better. We have cured diseases that killed millions of people. We have ended slavery in most of the world. We have technology that would seem like magic to people a hundred years ago.

In other ways, the world has gotten worse. We have weapons that can destroy the entire planet. We have pollution that is changing the climate. Many people feel lonelier and more anxious than people did in the past, even though they have more stuff.

Whether the world is "getting better" depends on what you think "better" means. And what you think "better" means depends on your beliefs about what matters most. A person who thinks technology is the most important thing will say the world is getting better. A person who thinks close community and connection with nature are the most important things might say it is getting worse.

"Progress" is not a fact. It is a belief about the direction of history. And like all beliefs, it shapes how people behave. If you believe progress is inevitable, you might be less worried about the future. If you believe it is not, you might work harder to protect what you already have.

### Part 4: Where Do Right and Wrong Come From?

This is one of the biggest questions in the world, and different people answer it very differently:

- **Religious answer:** Right and wrong come from God (or the gods, or the cosmic order). Stealing is wrong because God says so. The rules exist above human beings and cannot be changed by human decision.
- **Secular answer:** Right and wrong come from human reason and human agreement. Stealing is wrong because it hurts people and we have agreed that hurting people is wrong. The rules exist because we created them.

Notice what the secular answer requires: it requires you to believe that human beings can figure out right and wrong on their own, without help from God or a cosmic order. That is a big belief. It might be true. But it is a belief — just as much as the religious answer is.

Many people who think they "don't have beliefs about religion" actually have very strong beliefs about where right and wrong come from. They believe human beings can figure it out. They believe fairness matters. They believe hurting people is wrong. These are beliefs. They did not prove them scientifically. They believe them because they were raised in a culture that taught them these things — just as religious people were raised in cultures that taught them to believe in God.

### Part 5: What Is Fairness?

Everyone thinks fairness matters. But not everyone agrees on what fairness means.

- **Fairness as equal shares:** Everyone gets the same amount. If there are ten cookies and five people, everyone gets two. This sounds simple, but what if one person worked harder than the others? Should they still get only two?
- **Fairness as earned reward:** People who work harder get more. The person who baked the cookies gets more cookies. This sounds fair too — but what about the person who could not bake because they were sick? Do they get nothing?
- **Fairness as need:** People who need more get more. The hungry person gets more cookies than the person who just ate. This sounds compassionate — but it might also mean the person who worked hardest gets the least.

Each version of fairness makes sense. Each one comes from a different belief about what matters most: equality, effort, or need. People who argue about fairness are often arguing about which belief is right — without realizing that the argument is about beliefs, not about facts.

### Part 6: The Beliefs You Do Not Know You Have

Here is the hardest idea in this packet: the most powerful beliefs are the ones you do not know you have.

If someone asks "Do you believe in God?" you know that is a question about beliefs. You think about it and give an answer.

But if someone asks "Is it wrong to hurt people?" most people do not think of this as a question about beliefs. They think of it as obviously true — as a fact about the world. But it is not a fact in the way "water boils at 100 degrees Celsius" is a fact. It is a belief — a very important one, one most people share, but still a belief.

The purpose of this packet — and of all of 1.3 — is to help you see the beliefs that are usually invisible. Once you can see them, you can examine them. You can ask: "Do I believe this because I thought about it carefully, or because everyone around me believes it and I never questioned it?"

That question is the beginning of real thinking.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Rules Conversation [Early Packet]
Compare rules in different settings — home, school, a friend's house. Why are they different? What beliefs are behind each set of rules? The student practices identifying the belief behind the rule.

### Study 2: The Science Question [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Discuss: What can science tell us? What can it not tell us? Is "I believe in science" a complete worldview? What questions does it leave unanswered?

### Study 3: The Progress Question [Mid-Packet]
Discuss: Is the world getting better? Better in what ways? Worse in what ways? What does "better" mean? Who decides?

### Study 4: The Fairness Debate [Mid-Packet]
Use the cookie example (or a real situation). Discuss different versions of fairness. The student identifies the belief behind each version. There is no single right answer — the goal is to see that "fairness" depends on beliefs.

### Study 5: Invisible Beliefs [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Pick a common belief that most people treat as obvious ("people should be treated equally," "hurting people is wrong," "everyone deserves freedom"). Discuss: Is this a fact or a belief? Where did this belief come from? Could someone disagree and not be evil?

### Study 6: Comparing Worldviews [Late Packet]
Take a specific moral question (is it ever right to lie? should the strong help the weak?) and show how different worldviews answer it differently. The student sees that moral answers depend on starting beliefs.

### Study 7: What Do You Believe? [End of Packet]
The student identifies at least three beliefs they hold about what is right and wrong. For each, they try to explain where the belief came from. This is the beginning of conscious self-examination.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Belief Identification
**Developing well:** The student can identify beliefs behind rules and moral judgments. They spontaneously ask "but why do we think that?" about things previously taken for granted.
**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish between a fact and a belief.

### 2. The Science Distinction
**Developing well:** The student can name things science can explain and things it cannot, and understands that "I believe in science" is itself a belief.
**Developing slowly:** Treats science as the answer to all questions.

### 3. Self-Examination
**Developing well:** The student has identified at least one of their own beliefs and asked where it came from.
**Developing slowly:** Cannot or will not examine their own assumptions.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Making This Political
At Stretching Branches, this packet is about rules, fairness, and beliefs — not about political ideologies. Do not turn it into a critique of any political movement. The student is learning a skill (identifying beliefs) that will serve them across all political and ideological contexts.

### Implying That Secular Beliefs Are Wrong
The goal is not to debunk secularism. It is to make visible the beliefs that secular people hold. These beliefs may be true. They may be false. The point is that they are beliefs — not facts, not the absence of belief — and they deserve the same examination given to any other worldview.
